text: Intra-abdominal rectosigmoid transection with stump aversion can be an option during rectal surgery in men when rectal transection proves difficult.
The video demonstrates the rectal aversion technique with coloanal anastomosis being performed during a robotic-assisted ultra-low anterior resection.
The patient is a forty-nine-year-old male who is diagnosed with rectal cancer by colonoscopy and biopsy.
He underwent neoadjuvant chemotherapy and radiotherapy after complete staging of the lesion.
He then underwent a totally robotic ultra-low anterior resection using the Da Vinci S surgical system made by Intuitive Surgical, Sunnyvale, California, USA.
This was performed by a team of three surgeons.
The surgeon performs ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery and mesorectal dissection using a medial to lateral technique, visibly manipulating the surrounding tissue to achieve clear separation.
The surgeon completes the takedown of the splenic flexure and performs an anterior resection with total mesorectal excision, removing the rectal tissue while preserving surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses endoGI staplers to transect the proximal sigmoid colon and mesocolon, visibly separating these structures during the colorectal procedure.
After completing the transection, the surgeon confirms the adequate length of the proximal colon, visually assessing the segment for proper alignment and preparation for anastomosis.
A circular stapler with the anvil attached to the stapler head is introduced through the anus, advancing up to the end of the rectal stump.
The surgeon opens the circular stapler and places a suture around the gap between the stapler head and the anvil head, securing the suture around the anvil pike.
The top of the rectal stump is attached to the circular stapler, facilitating its withdrawal through the anus as the stapler is removed.
With the suture tied, the surgeon applies traction on the circular stapler, fully everting the rectal stump through the anus.
The surgeon performs distal transection at the level of the pectineal line, progressing until reaching the abdominal plane of dissection.
The surgeon removes the total mesorectal excision (TME) specimen from the surgical field, completing the resection process.
The surgeon removes the left colonic remnant through the anus and opens it, revealing the interior of the specimen.
The surgeon performs a hand-sewn coloanal anastomosis using interrupted absorbable sutures, with the surgical field exposed by a Lone Star retractor.
The surgeon reviews the abdominal cavity laparoscopically, focusing on the position of the colon within the cavity.
Diverting ileostomy was performed at the end of the procedure and the patient was discharged home well seven days postoperatively.
